text: The surgeon identifies and ligates the inferior mesenteric vein at its origin, beginning dissection near the trite ligament. The vein is isolated and clipped at its origin.

The surgeon detaches the mesocolon from the pancreatic edge and divides the torus fascia from the gyrotus plane. The peritoneal layer is opened towards the pelvis to identify the origin of the inferior mesenteric artery, which is isolated. The dissection proceeds to the sigmoidal artery, which is clipped at its origin while sparing the mesenteric artery.
The surgeon dissects the parietal peritoneum along the lateral margin of the colon, starting from the proximal sigmoid colon and extending towards the splenic flexure.

The surgeon performs a mesorectum dissection starting posteriorly, moving down into the pelvis. The posterior part is isolated, followed by lateral dissection to separate the rectal wings. Anteriorly, the peritoneum is incised one centimeter from the Douglas reflection to remove the Douglas pouch, and dissection continues in front of the denim VA's fascia.

The surgeon uses a robotic stapler to separate the distal rectum. Following the transection, endocyanin green fluorescence angiography is conducted to evaluate the vascularization, which is repeated after completing the colorectal anastomosis.
